description:
2.5 years and 10,000 kms Update

Sorry for being MIA for so long, with no major update in this thread since October ’23.

The car has been doing great, I was away at my work location in AP and the car wasn’t being driven much by my parents as well. By Mid-March the car was at the 9.6 - 9.7k kms mark.

I had to definitely be there when the car hit the 10k kms mark. I planned my trip to Mumbai in such a way that I would get my wisdom tooth extracted as the first thing when I land and also attend BMW’s JoyTown later in the week.

Subsequently, a trip to Pune was planned on the 15th, where I had a meeting. I clubbed the Pune plan with Lonavla, where I would be attending The Valley Run (Summer Edition) ‘24, as a spectator, being held at the Aamby Valley Air Strip.

With my wisdom tooth extracted on 6th April, I was on bed-rest till the 10th. After which I started going out and the first thing I did was getting the car’s interior and exterior professionally cleaned and waxed.

Got the work done at Wurth Car Care in Hiranandani Powai. Have mentioned the details and cost in this thread: https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/mumba...ai-mumbai.html (Wurth Car Care | Powai, Mumbai)



A picture post the work done.

Next, attended JoyTown on the 12th and 13th. This time the JoyTown was more concert-oriented, damn expensive and was organised by BookMyShowLive, the crowds were absolutely unimaginable on Saturday when Diljit Dosanjh was performing…





Enroute to the JoyTown




The F90 LCI M5 Duo were the showstoppers, standing outside on Day-1, they took part on Day-2 in the Drift Show.








The Toronto Red M4 on display was a real Magnet.


BMW’s Twilight Purple hits different when sunlight falls on it.


Me being a car-guy, I was more interested in the cars on display and the Drift Show that took place. If someone asks me name that one car that really took my breath away at the JoyTown event, then it has to be the XM, undoubtedly!

The exterior is a love-it or hate-it affair, but my god that interior is just something else !! The sheer quality, the sense of occasion, the soft leather used at all touch points, the headliner and sunroof really stood out and space on offer, really need to be experienced to believe it…









I had missed the Drift Show last year, so it was something I absolutely didn’t want to miss this year around. One of the many videos that I managed to capture of the Drift Show, for some reason the quality is not the best…

https://youtu.be/GoxxFeBTCzs?feature=shared





2x M4s and M2 were part of the Drift Show on Day-1

The Chainsmokers and Diljit’s performances were epic as well, but these were anyways secondary to me.



“The Chainsmokers”

BMW owners got some goodies if they presented their keys at the Merchandise section setup at the event. The goodies included a White JoyTown Cap (a Black JoyTown Cap was being given to the VIP and Lounge members), a transparent drink sipper with a BMW tag around it and some benefits when the car visits the service centre, which can be availed before 31st July 2024. Benefits include 20% discount on parts, accessories, discounts on the Pre-Monsoon check-up and the likes.

Since the car would be hitting the 10k kms mark immediately the next day, decided to buy a few official BMW accessories for the car to keep it fresh.

These included:
- Floating Wheel Caps (Set of 4)
- M Performance Key Cover
- BMW Logo Door Projector (the ///M Logo one)



Awaiting brick-bats, for getting the ///M logo accessories for a non-M car, in fact not even a M-Sport car . But nonetheless these small things will help keep the car fresh for a longer period of time. Also these parts are obnoxiously overpriced, I bought them only because they were being offered at a reasonable discount. Paid ~₹17k in total for these three parts.

Met a few family friends at JoyTown, who I didn’t know were also coming and the curtains dropped on the event. Two days gone by in a blink…

Meanwhile, I had booked our stay in Lonavla for the night of 14th of April, in such a way that I could attend The Valley Run ‘24 and also join the Expressway as soon as possible on 15th April morning for my meeting in Pune.

The car hit the 10,000km mark on the Eastern Expressway as we took the turn towards Mankhurd, heading towards Lonavla. We went through the Eastern Freeway, since I wanted to try out the new Atal Setu / MTHL. What a brilliant piece of engineering it is !!



The 10,000km milestone.

A quick stop for a cold coffee at the Food Mall before the Ghat starts, where I coincidentally managed to capture 3 Beamers standing side-by-side. I got down to capture mine and the Tanzanite Blue 6GT. The sun was scorching and I took the picture and went inside, it was only afterwards in the evening that I realised there was an M340i besides the 6GT as well.



”The Three (Beamers) Musketeers”

The Aamby Valley Air Strip was around 26-27kms from where we were staying. Checked into our hotel at 3PM, freshened up and left for the event at 3:45PM. The road leading to Aamby Valley is a joy to drive on and you can really hoon your car on the fast sweeping curves.

Reached the Air Strip and parked up our car where there was parking available and entered. The event like my friend had told me, just a day before, is always messed up in terms of following the time schedule. It must definitely be hard to manage it considering there are so many participants and events taking place together.

The bike races that were supposed to start at 10:30 - 11 AM in the morning started at 3:30 PM and of course everything after that was delayed as well. The Supercar Parade and 4W races were supposed to start at 4:30PM which I had actually gone to see but like mentioned everything was delayed.

Some pictures of the various cars at the Event, I couldn’t manage to click all of them…



McLaren Artura


India’s Fastest Pre-LCI M5


The subtle mods on this Hilux, really made me fall in love with one, including the Hot Wheels sticker! Now even I want a Hilux :’)


Imran Majid’s 1400hp Wild Child, that has a parachute attached at the back, to help stop it 


Another Godzilla R35


Sanam Sekhon’s (a.k.a India’s Drift King) 2JZ swapped Lexus GS 300.


Met Parth Gohil from the Motorbeam Team
(who is also a T-BHPian)

Met my friends who were present on both days of TVR ‘24, a major issue that they faced was the absolute non-availability of a washroom anywhere on the Airstrip, not even a temporary setup. I didn’t understand how can the organisers organise such a massive event where there are Female spectators and participants taking part as well, and yet not have a bloody washroom !!



We drove to the nearest washroom which was some 9kms away one-way. And this stretch is where I got to really hoon the X3. Oh, what joy! Sports mode activated, adaptive dampers stiffened and attacking each corner as it came… As I have mentioned earlier and in other threads as well Adaptive Dampers are a real game-changer, without the need of having the expensive Air-Suspension !!

There was lots of dust around the Air Strip, where we needed to park and the car was absolutely caked in it.



My Dust Trooper

I couldn’t stay for the entire duration of the event since my mother was worried about my father and I driving back from the event on those undivided and unlit roads. These roads also helped me understand the working of the Adaptive Headlights much better, including the Auto-Beam assist!! But the real event where the cars raced, I believe started post 8:30 - 9PM, which I missed. Reached our hotel by 8:10PM, had dinner by 9PM and retired for the day.

Next day since we had to head to Pune, woke up by 6:45AM, got ready, headed for breakfast at 7:30AM and checked-out of the hotel at 8AM. Joined the expressway in 5 mins flat and we were on our way to Pune.





The condition of the car, caked in dust, picture clicked the next morning.

Reached Pune by 10AM, completed my meeting by 11AM, met a college friend and his colleague at Viman Nagar with whom I had lunch and left for Mumbai.

On the way back took a slight detour through Pimpri-Chinchwad to take some photos of the JLR factory and TML PVBU gate where I had interned for 3 months in 2022. Refreshing some fond memories of one of the best 3 months I spent in Pune.



At TML’s PVBU Unit, Pimpri-Chinchwad

All the way we thought it was Sunday and why was there so much traffic entering Mumbai, we then realised it was a Monday, hence the office rush. Reached home by 7PM, got the car nicely washed for ₹250 and called it a day.

As I write this I’m on my way back to my remote work location in AP. Truth be told the last 4 days in Mumbai-Lonavla-Pune were really hectic but equally fun and rewarding !! Met a lot of car-enthusiasts and sharing the same mindset really helps in forming a bond.

By the end of the trip, the car has completed 10,400kms. The overall trip fuel economy was in the range of 10.5-11kmpl, which I consider decent for a full-time AWD petrol crossover.

Issues:- Now if many may remember that the car’s windscreen broke in Sept ‘23 and ever since I always find some shards of glass falling in the front passenger and driver footwell carpets. I’m guessing the glass pieces are still present somewhere inside the dashboard and whenever the car goes over any bad roads, these pieces fall down. Will have to get it checked when the car goes in for its service or for the Pre-Monsoon check-up. I’m sure this isn’t the first time Infinity would be changing a cracked windscreen, so I’m damn curious to understand how did they overlook such a critical point.

All in all, I’m mighty impressed by the package the X3 30i offers, it really took everything in its stride in a nonchalant manner. No roads, no problem. Very Dusty environment, absolutely no problem. Fast Sweeping Curves, no problem. Kind of becoming the One Car for all situations for me. The RFTs do make the ride stiff, and the car has a stiff edge to the way in which it handles bad roads, compared to its chief competitors the GLC, Q5 and XC60. Now only if the rear-seats could be reclined. Other than that I have absolutely no complaints. It’s neither too big nor too small as well for our road conditions. Just lovin’ the experience.

That’s all from my end this time, until the next update .

Quoting in this thread as I wanted some advice on flex films for BMw X3 windshield .

My X3 also had a stone falling on 5 spots , although tiny they are an eyesore when one drives in specific lighting conditions .

BMW dealers will replace that on windshield however I read in online forums that a flex film does protect the windshield . The BMW dealership told me that it’s a 0.5 mm thick film and with the wipers on during monsoon spells , the film would last 2 years , cost Rs 20K .

Compared to the damage I felt the cost of using one is worth it .

Any advice on this subject by experts ?

description:
11,000km Update, Minor Accessories added and Experiencing a Porsche

Not a major update like previous ones, but the car completed 11000kms in July.



After coming back from Goa, fixed up a visit to Infinity Turbhe to get the Nabenabdeckung Feststehend aka Floating Centre Caps and BMW LED Door Projectors fitted.



The Accessories bought at the JoyTown ‘24 Event


The BMW logo projecting on the floor looks spectacular


The Doggo intrigued by the Floating Wheel Caps

My philosophy is simple, adding some minor touches to the car as it ages like fine wine, to keep the freshness and attachment intact.

I had bought these accessories at a discounted price at the BMW JoyTown ‘24 festival. Where the pop-up stall run had noted down all my details Name, Car’s registration number, Dealer from which the car was purchased, phone number, email ID and few other details so that they could link it to the respective dealerships and service centres.

When I reached Infinity Turbhe in the afternoon on 18th July, and got an advisor assigned, he told me whether I got a quote for the accessories beforehand, to which I mentioned I already have the accessories with me. Till this point the accessories were kept in the car, to which the advisor said we can’t fit aftermarket accessories. I got the accessories from the car and showed it to the advisor as being Genuine BMW Accessories.

The advisor wanted a bill for the accessories I had brought and from where did I procure them. When I told him JoyTown, he had no clue what JoyTown was or acted as if he didn’t?! Can’t believe there can be such a big disconnect between the service centre and BMW India’s official event.

Since I didn’t have the bill, the advisor mentioned sorry we can’t install the same and a Job card can’t be opened for the same. Thankfully I had the credit card’s transaction slip which mentioned Infinity Cars Worli/Lalbaug as the seller, which somewhat convinced him that these were genuine accessories I had brought with me.

Without a Job Card, the installation was carried out, and a manual gate pass was given to me, to be submitted at the gate. The Advisor didn’t even have the courtesy to show me what was fitted.

Still can’t believe such a massive communication gap between Infinity’s Sales Team at JoyTown and their Service Centre can exist. What were the details taken down for at JoyTown then, I guess for spamming their promotional activities. Anyways didn’t have a good experience at all, compared to my previous visits to the Turbhe facility.

Moving On; was scheduled to meet a Car-Enthusiast friend from Gurgaon who had come down to Navi Mumbai after completing my visit to Infinity Turbhe. Coincidentally he was also at Infinity Turbhe in the morning for the service of his Porsche Cayenne.

Met him and drove to a peaceful spot to admire the cars. Took turns driving each other’s prized possessions, pointing out the delicate intricacies in both cars.

Before the Cayenne my friend had a 2019 X5 30d and it was interesting to hear what was better in the X5 and what is better in the Cayenne. It’s true I guess when we hear that Porsche is the next logical upgrade from BMW, granted you can afford it and I won’t disagree.

Some details on this specific Cayenne before, I proceed to the ‘Porsche’ experience.

2023 Porsche Cayenne Platinum Edition (Model Code: 9YA)
- 2996cc V6 Petrol
- 335hp and 450Nm
- 8 speed PDK gearbox
- 90L Fuel Tank (optional; Standard is 75L)
- Exterior: Moonlight Blue Metallic
- Interior: Partial Leather Black and Mojave Beige (Optioned)
- 14 Way Electrically Adjustable Comfort Seats with Memory Presets (Optional)
- 21 inch RS Spyder Design wheels in Satin Platinum (Optional)
- Sport Chrono Pack incl. Analogue Clock on the dashboard and Mode Selector on the Steering (Optional)
- Black Out package
- Quad blackened Exhaust Tips (Optional)

The last time I sat in the Driver’s Seat of a Porsche was in 2015, in a Display Cayman, at the Porsche Museum in Stuttgart. This was my first time driving a Porsche.

Coming from a X3, you will immediately realise how much more substantial the Cayenne is size-wise. Even-though the Cayenne is a size bigger than the X3, like what the X5 is, it feels almost two sizes larger when viewed head-on, courtesy the upright grill and massive bonnet design unique to Porsches.








Porsche’s Moonlight Blue Metallic is very similar to BMW’s Carbon Black; where the blue shows in very specific lighting conditions. The 21 inch RS Spyder Design alloys complement the Cayenne’s design perfectly!






Inside you are welcomed by the massive comfort seats upfront with a multitude adjustments (14 Way) possible. The cabin cocoons you fantastically well and the sheer quality is mind blowing. This particular car isn’t optioned with the Extended Leather Package, which would’ve have made the interior even more special! The optioned Mojave Beige interior brings a lot of airiness inside the cabin. The interior quality is just fabulous and feels one level above an X5.



Coming to the Driver’s Seat, you realise why a Porsche is a Porsche ! The Analogue Rev Counter is what catches your attention right when you enter the driver’s seat! The centred analogue rev counter is what gives Porsche its unique identity. Physical buttons are present in the car (many have become touch on the Facelift) and everything is within the Driver’s reach.



You don’t get a traditional push button start, what you get instead is a turnable manual key that can’t be removed. Pretty unique…

The engine is very refined and you’ll almost not believe that the engine is turned on. The Sports Chrono Pack gets you the mode selector on the Steering itself and makes it easy to switch modes without taking your eyes off the road.

The Steering feels very special to hold and is so damn connected, very different to BMW steerings and in a good way. BMW has started dialling in some artificial weight into their steerings, none of that present in Porsche.



Can’t get enough of this steering wheel with every Car Enthusiast’s most revered Logo and Aluminium Paddle shifters

The way the V6 pulls is just something else! Eventhough it’s not a diesel the lusty petrol power makes up for the diesel torque. Sport Chrono pack makes the car quicker compared to cars without it and the Launch Control is absolutely addictive, do it multiple times and still each time the system hooks up brilliantly giving you the perfect launch. You’re pinned back into the seat on launch and you hit triple digit speeds very quickly for a 2 Ton+ burly SUV.

The X3 in comparison feels like a sedan after sitting in the Cayenne  The Cayenne even with RFTs and 21 inch wheels rounds off bad roads with aplomb something that really surprised me, my X3 with 19 inch RFTs feels stiffer in Comfort mode than the Cayenne, all courtesy to the Porsche Active Suspension Management (PASM) in the Cayenne.

I also found out that the Pre-LCI X3 in Luxury Line gets Launch Control, much to my surprise. Although it’s very temperamental and activated just once and never again how much ever we tried. My friend was reminded of his X5 after driving my X3, just slightly smaller in size. The B48 2.0L (in 30i guise) 252hp petrol motor is extremely potent and continues to keep me satisfied.

Had a grin driving all the way back to Powai from Palm Beach Road, of course it doesn’t have a 6 cylinder engine or is a Porsche, but the perfect size for Mumbai traffic along with more than sufficient power is what makes it a special package for me. Special mention to the Adaptive Suspension, which stiffens up the suspension when you really want to have fun without having the complex Air Suspension !



The car turns 3 soon and these accessories give it some freshness that will go a long way in making the car feel special. The 3rd Service is also coming this month, as soon as the car indicates it.

Part of the reason for the even lower running is the new entrant into the garage 
https://www.team-bhp.com/forum/test-...ml#post5787680
title: 241183 blue bolt our bmw x3 30i ownership review 3 years 9 months 16 300kms completed

description:
3 Years 9 Months & 16,300 kms Update

Haven’t updated the thread in a while, due to multiple reasons, so here it goes.

After the last update in August ‘24, the car went in for its scheduled Brake Fluid replacement in Aug ‘24 and 3rd Service in Sept ‘24 at Infinity Turbhe. Why two visits in consecutive months, well all due to the delay in the first service at Infinity Lalbaug causing the engine oil change and brake fluid service dates to get changed by a month. Have never visited the Lalbaug service centre again.



At Infinity Turbhe for the Brake Fluid Service


On the hoist, for the Brake Fluid Service

Although the Service Advisor assured me that there will be no issue if Brake Fluid Service is delayed by a month, I didn’t want to risk it considering later on they might turn around and say that you’ve exceeded the service by a month, now your warranty and service pack is void. Something which Lalbaug had tried at the first service itself which was completely their fault anyways since they couldn’t accommodate the car when the service was due.

Due to this experience right in the first year, the Infinity CRM gave a complimentary Interior Detailing service which I took up in this service.



The rear underbody and exhaust


The underbody from the front, everything is neatly covered with protection


The Front McPherson Suspension, competition uses the more expensive double wishbone setups


The rear suspension, can see the adaptive damper control


Spotted DBHPian karan561 Sir’s E46 328i at Infinity Turbhe, back in Aug ‘24

The RFTs simply suck! They haven’t left me stranded anywhere but the inherent stiffness can get on your nerves sometimes. Also, I am experiencing some swaying whenever the car hits one of those infamous patch-work that has been done on Mumbai roads. I am not sure is it swaying due to the RFTs or is there some other underlying issue, because whenever it does so, it’s necessarily to hold the steering wheel with a very firm grip!

My father has been alternating between the X3 and the HyCross for his daily office commute and he is really frustrated with the rear AC of the X3. According to him unless you turn the front blower to the Max and drop the temperature to 20-21Deg C, it becomes tough to sit at the rear. Although BMW calls this a 3 Zone AC, I would term it as a 2 Zone AC because just with a temperature control at the rear, the fan speed is dependent on the front 2 Zones only. I would’ve really appreciated a Fan Speed Control and AC Vents on the B-Pillars for the rear seats, like how it is in the 5 series and X5. My father has sat in his friend’s 3GL and there he was much happier with the AC at the backseat, I hope there isn’t anything wrong with our X3, considering German cars usually have weaker ACs compared to other manufacturers.

In terms of driving dynamics, this car I feel is still unmatched! The powertrain is the sweet spot, the B48 in 30i guise and ZF8 always in sync and have almost negligible lag.

I was invited by Infinity Worli to test-drive and check-out the new G45 X3, I was in town last month and decided to go down to the showroom. I requested a test-drove of the X3 20 (20i) so I could draw a fair comparison between my G01 X3 30i and the new G45 X3 20. I drove down via the Sea-link and Coastal road to the showroom and tested the new X3 on the same stretch.



G45 and G01 X3s together, the G45 is imposing and unmistakable for anything else


The G45 X3’s rear profile is distinctive as well, with the number plate being shifted to the bumper

The drop in power, drop in interior quality and lack of premium touches on the interior and yet paying 20 Lakhs premium over the previous G01 X3 is something I just couldn’t fathom! Okay, BMW has added some serious amount of tech like L2 ADAS, Virtual Navigation, Comfort Access, Inbuilt-dashcam with 360 Deg recording option, etc. but BMW has gone softer on the driving dynamics aspect and is making up where they have softened with added technology. The exact same thing was observed with the U11 X1 as well when it was launched in India.

Even something as basic as the headlight protection in the engine bay of the G45 is missing, which is present in the G01 X3



G01’s engine bay, where the headlights are protected by the plastic panel on top


The G45’s engine bay gets no such plastic panel on top of the headlights


What I Loved in the G45 X3

- Looks distinctive and is well differentiated from the competition
- Absolutely loaded with tech, making the older G01 X3 almost feel ancient in terms of tech
- Ventilated front seats were a real boon in Mumbai’s humid weather
- RFTs have been ditched for regular tubeless Vredestein tires wrapping the cool looking alloys
- Good to see the Adaptive Dampers have been retained and they work as intended


What I Loathed in the G45 X3

- Absolutely pathetic quality of plastic used throughout the cabin, the switchgear just doesn’t justify the premium price tag compared to the aluminium switchgear in my G01 X3
- Front seats felt narrow and more body hugging compared to the G01’s front seats
- The dashboard and door cards are covered in quilted fabric, which feels horrible to touch and will be loaded with dust in no time
- Feels very expensive at 90-92L OTR
- The 190hp Petrol is underpowered for the price when compared to competitors like the GLC 300 and even the age old Q5 45 TFSI. The 20i spec feels strained once you start pushing it
- Everything is jammed into the touchscreen making it an cumbersome task to operate some very basic functionalities as well

Immediately after the G45 X3 20, I drove the LCI G05 X5 40i and absolutely fell head-over-heels with it! That inline 6-cylinder B58 engine making 381hp and 520Nm is an absolute peach, with the ride height lowered in Sports mode, the car was cornering like it was on rails on the Coastal Road connectors. This is one car I would really like to have in the future, a new one is out-of-reach considering the insane asking price of 1.3 Cr OTR Mumbai, but a pre-owned X5 40i is something I would really dig. Also space-wise the backseat felt very similar to the X3 (both G45 & G01) other than the height of the seat, a GLE’s rear seat is far more spacious and the backrest angle in the X5 is quite upright which will not suit everyone’s taste. The X5 along with the 3 series are the last good-looking BMWs on sale, not long before they get ruined with a generation change as well.


What I Loved in the X5

- Silky smooth B58 which is the real gem, masks the 2Ton+ weight with aplomb
- One of the best looking BMW in today’s market scene
- Air suspension makes ingress and egress fairly easy
- Loaded with tech like 360 deg dashcam after the LCI Update
- Split tailgate looks special and utility-wise can take upto 300kgs load
- Ventilated front seats were a real boon in Mumbai’s humid weather
- 4 Zone AC with 2 AC vents each for 4 passengers and separate Fan controls allows each passenger to have their own setting
- Superior quality of materials used all around in the cabin


What I Loathed in the X5

- Damn expensive at 1.3Cr OTR
- Backseat isn’t spacious compared to the GLE and feels similar in space to the smaller X3, which in turn feels similar to the smaller X1 
- The rear seat backrest angle is very upright
- Air suspension is expensive to replace when it fails, even the XLine trim now gets Air Suspension
- 21 inch tires will be expensive to replace if damaged and while replacing as well
- The front AC Vents are difficult to direct where we want them to actually throw air
- Everything is jammed into the touchscreen making it an cumbersome task to operate some very basic functionalities as well



Really fell head-over-heels for the X5, the design really is something else


With the LCI lack of physical buttons is sorely felt, with everything being jam-packed into the touchscreen


The Rear seat of the X5 felt very similar in space to the smaller X3 and the backrest angle is a bit too upright

The X5 blows the new X3 out of the water, the differentiation that BMW has managed to create between the two almost justifies the price difference of 40 Lakhs between the two.

Meanwhile the new G45 X3 just reinforces my decision to keep our G01 X3 for a much longer time! Not only is the engine more potent, but even the quality of the switchgear used is superior. Only thing missing is the latest technology, which anyways I’m not a big fan of, except Comfort Access and the features present in the BMW App.

Also got to see the new G68 5 series with the optional 19 inch alloys and Aero kit worth 11 Lakhs at the Infinity Worli Showroom:


And the G90 M5 in Marina Bay Blue Metallic with a Merino Red/Black interior:


Some pictures of Blue Bolt over past few months



With its G01 Phytonic Blue brother in LCI MSport guise, pick your poison




Have started appreciating the older design language of BMW a lot more, since the new generation models have come in


With Mumbai’s skyline


Christmas 2024




At Malabar Hill just 2 weeks back


Parked beside the car, in the exact same specification, which we almost bought back in 2021: GLC 200 in Cavansite Blue

2 days before leaving for my workplace, decided to meet up with a IG friend and his Pre-LCI G05 X5 40i finished in Tanzanite Blue.

Drove from the Airport till Bandra Bandstand. With automobile enthusiasts, we bond over cars and it’s spontaneous, no shyness or anything interfering our car-talks 



One of my new favourite photographs, worth framing according to me


”The eyes, Chico, they never lie”


The Laser lights of the X5 with the blue elements look breathtaking at night-time


The X5’s interior is a place where you could spend hours together, without getting bored. I prefer the ambient interior lighting of the Pre-LCI X5




Once in a while filling up XP95, the car has been returning around 8kmpl in city conditions, haven’t taken it out on the expressway for quite sometime now

That’s all from my side in this update, had gotten busy with the Innova’s thread and almost forgot that this thread existed!

description:
If the decision is going to be on macro factors like - plush interiors, luxurious feel, feeling that you are buying a car worth it's money, better brand value (hardly anyone buys the new X3) then GLC 300 it is.

When it comes to micro factors, there are various things that you can consider that comes with the X3. Again, these aren't deal breakers. But consider these based on how much you value these.

1. A new gen car, breaks the monotony of the family looks
2. Adaptive suspension with comfort and sport modes. (GLC's ride is stiffer)
3. Adaptive LED headlights with cornering function (GLC has reflector type, without cornering function)
4. 10 seconds boost button on the paddles, handy for quick overtakes
5. Driver seat bolstering adjustment
6. Google Maps directions in the instrument cluster
7. Cooling pad for wireless charger
8. Staggered tyres
9. Better rear seats compared to the GLC
10. Overall, better driveability
11. NFC key, smartphones can be the key.

On the GLC side, these are the ones:

1. Off-road mode gives the transparent bonnet view, can come in handy
2. Larger boot (620L vs 570L)
3. Seat kinetics, which helps relieve some stress during extended drives
4. Fingerprint based profile settings

Some quirks:

1. The rear camera in the GLC pops down every time a vehicle comes close or you engage the reverse gear. It makes a noise which you need to get used to.
2. GLC has 2 zone A/C vs 3-zone A/C of the X3.
3. The sunroof cover in the GLC is thin and in a lighter shade, can let in more heat, impacting AC performance on hot days. Having 5 vents in the front helps. On the other hand, the X3's sunroof cover is in a darker shade, but it's AC vents are too narrow, hence it reduces the air flow, you may have to run the AC in higher fan speeds to compensate (can get noisy).
4. X3's menu is not intuitive to use like the GLC. More scrolling and touches needed to get to a specific setting.

description:
I am sure the X3 30i is going to be much better to drive than GLC 300. So if driving pleasure is the main, go for X3. The GLC interiors give a sense of luxury more than X3, but quality of interiors are not up to the mark for the price. Biggest downer for me is the artificial leather seats in GLC, for this reason alone I wouldn’t choose GLC. Not sure how it is in new X3.

Though I am not a big fan of X3 looks, I don’t find the GLC round shape to be great too.

Suggest you take a test drive of X3 30i and decide.

Also BMW has invited for an off-road event of X3 in first week of March. I guess those who have already attended this event in Pune or Mumbai can share some feedback.

description:

description:
Maybe we should start a poll on this 🙂

While design is always subjective, my pick would be the GLC 300. For me, it stands out because of the luxurious cabin experience and its well-proportioned. It simply looks like a proper luxury SUV.

On the other hand, the new X3’s exterior feels a bit like an overgrown hatchback. The previous-gen X3, in my opinion had far better proportions and a much stronger road presence.

As an overall package though, the GLC 300 makes a compelling case, especially since it also drives well, which should make the decision easier.

description: If I am buying a 90 lakh car, it should feel like a 90 lakh car. Either in terms of luxury or in terms of power. Only the GLC feel luxurious here. If driving dynamics matter, you should be looking at a sedan, not an SUV. Get the GLC if you are after luxury or an m340i if you are after power.
title: 305244 mercedes glc 300 bmw x3 30i

description:
The GLC 300 is a better product overall, thanks to its plush interiors, superior plastic quality, and better cabin design. The new generation X3, on the other hand, doesn’t look great—at least to me. Its interior is way too plasticky and doesn’t feel like a luxury car.

While the X3 is known for driving pleasure, the GLC 300 is not far behind. When you’re spending almost ₹90 lakhs, it’s better to buy a car that feels worth the price. If you can’t stretch your budget for an X5, I’d suggest looking at the BMW 330Li. It will save you some money, and it has better driving dynamics than the X3 as well as better interiors. But if you ask me to choose between the X3 and the GLC, I will vote for the GLC.

description:
I request opinion of BHPians about the rear tyres of X3 30.

After reading a comment in another thread I checked for the availability of 285/40 R20 tyres online. I was surprised that in all online platforms just one option (Pirelli) is available. This made me think that if anything happens to these low profile tyres on any outstation trip we will not have any option but to tow the car back home.

Moreover even at home (Goa for me) the availability will be a problem. It might take weeks if not months to arrange for these tyres.
Because of this I have almost dropped X3.


I shall be highly grateful if others can share their opinion on this topic.
title: 305244 mercedes glc 300 bmw x3 30i

description:
Quote:
Originally Posted by Amith_kumar 
I request opinion of BHPians about the rear tyres of X3 30.

I shall be highly grateful if others can share their opinion on this topic.
If I were you, I would take extended test drives of the X3 and see how much like it vis-à-vis the GLC or whatever else you have in mind.

If you really do end up liking it, here's something you can consider; the X3 internationally offers an option for 19-inch wheels, with a tire profile of 245/50/19. Explore the option of trading the 20s in for 19s from the dealer itself. It can be done in the aftermarket too, for less than you'd pay the dealer, but then the dealer will wail about you losing the warranty to spook you.

19s with the extra sidewall would be a lot more suited to your needs. And even if you do stick with the 20s, you won't need to tow it back home. You'll just have to fit the space saver tire, and drive around slowly until such time as you change back to the full spec.

As well, I don't think you'd have to worry about the availability of 19 and 20 inch tyres. Even when I have run into issues sourcing tires in an 18-inch size, I have sourced it from Bengaluru, and it gets here in a day or two. Same if I were to source it from Delhi. You too can expand your net and source tires from either of these three markets, it's not nearly as much of a hassle as you might think it to be. Prices are competitive, and shipping charges are reasonable enough.

Take my advice with a pinch of salt, and under advisement. Don't eliminate the X3 if you really like it just because of the tire situation.
